investigation.investigation_summary:
SEEK Limited (SEK.AX) fell 5.85% on 22 May 2026 to close at A$12.71, touching a new 52-week low of A$12.70 during the session **(CNBC, 22 May 2026)**. The decline extends a severe downtrend that has cut the share price by more than 46% from its September 2025 high of A$29.84 **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)**. Despite the magnitude of the move, no company-specific press release, trading update, or ASX filing dated 21–22 May 2026 was identified that directly explains the drop. The company’s investor centre displayed the 22 May price and volume data but did not list a same-day ASX announcement in the page content reviewed ***(SEEK investor centre, 22 May 2026)***.

Earlier in May, an ASX disclosure showed that BlackRock Group ceased to be a substantial holder in SEEK as of 30 April 2026 ***(ASX/Listcorp, 3 May 2026)***. Trading volume on 21 May was reported to have exceeded the daily average by roughly 28% **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)**, suggesting some acceleration in selling pressure heading into the 22 May session. The most recent fundamental update was the H1 FY26 result in mid-February, which showed adjusted profit growth but a statutory loss driven by a A$356 million impairment of the Zhaopin investment, alongside soft paid job-ad volumes in Australia & New Zealand *(Rask Media, 17 Feb 2026)*. With no identifiable new catalyst for the specific 22 May move, the selling appears most consistent with continued negative sentiment and technical breakdown in a stock already under sustained institutional and macro pressure.
established_facts: SEK.AX fell 5.85% to close at A$12.71 on 22 May 2026 and touched a new 52-week low of A$12.70 during the session **(CNBC, 22 May 2026)**.
The stock has declined 46.37% from its 52-week high of A$29.84 set on 23 September 2025 **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)**.
An ASX filing disclosed that BlackRock Group ceased to be a substantial holder in SEEK as of 30 April 2026 ***(ASX/Listcorp, 3 May 2026)***.

SEEK's H1 FY26 result, reported in February 2026, included a statutory loss of A$178 million driven by a A$356 million impairment of its Zhaopin investment, and paid job-ad volumes in Australia & New Zealand dipped slightly *(Rask Media, 17 Feb 2026)*.
On 21 May 2026, SEK.AX trading volume exceeded its daily average by approximately 27.6% **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)**.

decision.rationale:
Call 1 found no company-specific catalyst for the 5.85% drop on 22 May 2026. SEEK’s investor centre showed no same-day ASX announcement ***(SEEK investor centre, 22 May 2026)***, and the decline extended a 46% fall from the September 2025 high **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)**. With volume elevated **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)** but no new disclosure, the move appears to reflect continued technical selling and sentiment deterioration rather than a material new fact.

The only substantive fundamental change identified—the BlackRock substantial-holder exit effective 30 April—was already disclosed on 3 May ***(ASX/Listcorp, 3 May 2026)***, nearly three weeks before the price drop. Likewise, the February H1 FY26 impairment and soft ANZ job-ad volumes *(Rask Media, 17 Feb 2026)* were long-digested. No Stage 4 thesis exists, so there are no structural assumptions to challenge, and without an anchor price the >15% cumulative-move threshold cannot be tied to a stale valuation.

Given the low investigation confidence and the absence of high-credibility new information, a rerun would be driven purely by price action already captured by the mechanical trigger. Defaulting to no-rerun avoids spending compute on speculative, backward-looking sentiment that has already been processed by the market.

SEEK’s investor centre did not list a same-day ASX announcement on 22 May 2026 ***(SEEK investor centre, 22 May 2026)***
BlackRock Group ceased to be a substantial holder in SEEK as of 30 April 2026 ***(ASX/Listcorp, 3 May 2026)***
H1 FY26 result included a statutory loss driven by a A$356 million impairment of the Zhaopin investment alongside soft paid job-ad volumes in Australia & New Zealand *(Rask Media, 17 Feb 2026)*
Trading volume on 21 May 2026 exceeded the daily average by approximately 27.6% **(FT.com, 22 May 2026)**

decision.considered_alternative: The alternative is to rerun because the stock has fallen 46% from its high, hit a new 52-week low on elevated volume, and lacks an existing thesis, implying the pipeline might need to build a new valuation from scratch. Yet a rerun based solely on price action and stale news would likely reproduce the same information already known, violating the bias toward no-rerun when evidence is speculative and already public.

investigation.investigation_summary: On 22 May 2026, SEEK Limited (ASX: SEK) closed down 5.85% at A$12.71, touching an intraday low of A$12.70 on volume of 1,320,440 shares ***(SEEK Limited investor centre, 22 May 2026)***. The decline extended a severe year-to-date drawdown that pushed the stock to its lowest level in roughly a decade *(The Bull, 22 May 2026)*. A May 22 market commentary piece attributed the day's weakness to strategic setbacks, including the company's recently announced plans to exit its Employment Hero stake and a tightening of its longer-term FY26 outlook, stating that these developments triggered an immediate negative share price reaction *(The Bull, 22 May 2026)*. However, the underlying strategic decisions—the commencement of the Employment Hero divestment and the tightening of FY26 guidance—were originally disclosed by SEEK on 17 February 2026 ***(Employment Hero press release, 17 Feb 2026; AFR company announcement PDF, 17 Feb 2026)***. The same May 22 report also noted that broker Citi had recently flagged concerns about management commentary on ad volumes and implications for FY26-FY27 forecasts following a results call, which reportedly added to selling pressure *(The Bull, 22 May 2026)*. Additional headwinds cited include an ongoing writedown related to SEEK's Chinese platform Zhaopin and structural competitive threats from LinkedIn and AI-powered recruiting solutions *(The Bull, 22 May 2026)*. It is not definitively established whether the 22 May drop was sparked by a discrete new announcement or by cumulative market digestion of these existing pressures.
established_facts: SEK.AX closed at A$12.71 on 22 May 2026, down 5.85%, with an intraday low of A$12.70 and volume of 1,320,440 shares ***(SEEK Limited investor centre, 22 May 2026)***.

SEEK announced on 17 February 2026 that its Growth Fund commenced a process to divest its stake in Employment Hero, targeting a liquidity window in calendar 2026 ***(Employment Hero press release, 17 Feb 2026)***.
SEEK tightened its FY26 guidance on 17 February 2026 to net revenue of $1.19bn–$1.23bn and EBITDA of $530m–$550m ***(AFR company announcement PDF, 17 Feb 2026)***.

A 22 May 2026 commentary piece reported that SEEK shares hit a decade low and cited strategic pressures including the Employment Hero exit, Zhaopin writedowns, and competitive threats from LinkedIn and AI as weighing on the stock *(The Bull, 22 May 2026)*.
Broker Citi was reported to have raised concerns about SEEK management commentary on ad volumes and FY26-FY27 forecasts after a recent results call *(The Bull, 22 May 2026)*.

candidate_causes.supporting_evidence: The Bull (22 May 2026) notes official data showing online job ads running materially below prior-year levels and describes market skepticism that yield initiatives can sustainably offset falling volumes.

decision.rationale: Call 1 attributes the 22 May decline primarily to the repricing of strategic risks—specifically the Employment Hero divestment and tightened FY26 guidance—that SEEK originally disclosed on 17 February 2026 (***, Employment Hero press release; ***, AFR company announcement PDF). Because these drivers were already known to the market for more than three months, the price action appears to reflect cumulative digestion of existing information rather than a reaction to a discrete new material event. The one potentially newer factor, a Citi broker note raising concerns about ad volumes and FY26-FY27 forecasts, is sourced solely to The Bull (*) and could not be independently verified by Call 1 against primary or established outlets. References to Zhaopin writedowns and competitive threats from LinkedIn/AI are similarly carried only by The Bull (*). With no existing Stage 3 or Stage 4 thesis to challenge, and no high-credibility evidence of a fresh catalyst, the investigation does not clear the threshold for an expensive pipeline rerun. Call 1 itself rates its confidence as medium and explicitly flags ambiguity over whether the drop stems from a new announcement or ongoing repricing, which under the decision framework counsels toward inaction.
key_facts_relied_on: SEEK announced on 17 February 2026 that its Growth Fund commenced a process to divest its stake in Employment Hero, targeting a liquidity window in calendar 2026 (***, Employment Hero press release, 17 Feb 2026)
SEEK tightened its FY26 guidance on 17 February 2026 to net revenue of $1.19bn–$1.23bn and EBITDA of $530m–$550m (***, AFR company announcement PDF, 17 Feb 2026)
Broker Citi was reported to have raised concerns about SEEK management commentary on ad volumes and FY26-FY27 forecasts after a recent results call (*, The Bull, 22 May 2026)
It is unclear whether the 22 May drop was driven by a discrete new company announcement or by cumulative market digestion of previously known strategic issues (Call 1 ambiguity notes)
decision.uncertainty_acknowledged: The decision is complicated by the severity of the price action—a decade low on a major signal—and the possibility that an unverified Citi note reflects a material incremental deterioration in ad volumes. However, the inability to confirm that note with primary or established sources, combined with the lack of an existing thesis to anchor the analysis, means the evidence is too speculative to justify rerun costs. If the cumulative drawdown exceeds 15% from anchor and fresh primary-source confirmation of the broker concerns emerges, the calculus would shift.
decision.considered_alternative: The case for rerun rests on the major price signal pushing SEK to decade lows and the possibility that the Citi ad-volume commentary—if accurate—signals structural earnings impairment not yet priced in, which would be valuable to evaluate afresh in a new thesis. However, this case depends on unverified, weakly sourced secondary reporting and treats a cumulative repricing of old news as a new event, which is insufficient under the default-no-rerun standard.
